title: Level 1: Hello World (LED Blinking)
aim: To write an Embedded C program to blink an LED connected to an ATmega328P.
objective: Understand GPIO configuration using DDRx and PORTx registers, and learn how to stall the MCU using delay routines.
theory:
What is GPIO?

General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins allow microcontrollers to read digital signals (like buttons) or write digital signals (like turning on an LED).

The Registers

  • DDRx (Data Direction Register): Configures a pin as INPUT (0) or OUTPUT (1). For example, setting DDRB = 0xFF makes all PORTB pins outputs.
  • PORTx (Data Register): When a pin is configured as an output, writing a 1 to PORTx sets the pin HIGH (5V), and writing a 0 sets it LOW (0V).

The Delay Loop

Microcontrollers operate infinitely fast (up to 16 million instructions per second on an Arduino UNO). If you turn an LED ON and OFF without a delay, it happens so fast that it will just look dimly lit. A delay function forces the microcontroller to count empty cycles, keeping the LED in its current state long enough for the human eye to see.

pretest.question: Which register is responsible for setting a pin as an Input or Output?
pretest.explanation: The Data Direction Register (DDR) configures pin direction.

pretest.question: If you write '1' to a bit in the PORT register while the DDR bit is set to '1', what happens to the output pin?
pretest.explanation: Writing 1 to PORT when DDR is 1 drives the pin HIGH.

procedure: Connect the Anode (long leg) of an LED to Pin 13 (PORTB Pin 5) of the ATmega328P.
Connect the Cathode (short leg) of the LED through a 330Ω resistor to GND.
Write the code to set DDRB bit 5 as an output.
In the infinite loop, set PORTB bit 5 HIGH.
Call a delay function to wait for 1000ms.
Set PORTB bit 5 LOW, and wait another 1000ms.

posttest.question: Why do we need a resistor connected in series with the LED?
options: To limit current and prevent burning out the LED
posttest.explanation: LEDs have no internal resistance, so they will draw infinite current and burn out without a current-limiting resistor.

feedback: Congratulations! You successfully configured an output pin and wrote a blocking loop to blink the LED.
